title: WITTIG REACTION
summary: Overview page for the Wittig reaction describing conversion of carbonyl compounds into alkenes through phosphonium ylides, with attention to stabilized, semi-stabilized, and unstabilized ylides, betaine/oxaphosphetane intermediates, and E/Z selectivity patterns.
notes: Summarizes phosphonium salt formation, ylide generation, oxaphosphetane collapse, and the effects of ylide class and solvent on alkene stereochemistry.

title: WITTIG REACTION
summary: Application page for the Wittig reaction featuring highly Z-selective coupling in discodermolide synthesis, Horner-Wittig olefination in buflavin synthesis, iterative olefination in oligosaccharide assembly, and late-stage alkene installation in complex natural products.
notes: Examples emphasize chemoselective alkene construction from aldehydes with phosphonium or phosphonate reagents in advanced synthetic settings.

title: WITTIG REACTION - SCHLOSSER MODIFICATION
summary: Overview page for the Schlosser modification of the Wittig reaction describing one-pot conversion of nonstabilized phosphorus ylides and carbonyl compounds into predominantly E-alkenes through lithiobetaine equilibration and selective collapse.
notes: Highlights lithiobetaine and beta-oxido phosphorane equilibria, cleavage with excess organolithium, and stereochemical bias toward E-alkenes.

title: WITTIG REACTION - SCHLOSSER MODIFICATION
summary: Application page for the Schlosser modification highlighting E-selective alkene formation in the syntheses of ISP-I, dolatrienoic acids, muricatacin, and sphingosine stereoisomers.
notes: Examples exploit Schlosser conditions to reverse or sharpen alkene geometry relative to classical Wittig conditions.

title: WITTIG-[1,2]- AND [2,3]-REARRANGEMENT
summary: Overview page for the Wittig [1,2]- and [2,3]-rearrangements describing base-induced rearrangement of aryl alkyl ethers and allylic ethers through alpha-alkoxy carbanions to furnish alcohol or homoallylic alcohol products.
notes: Contrasts radical-pair [1,2]-Wittig rearrangement with concerted sigmatropic [2,3]-Wittig rearrangement and their stereochemical outcomes.

title: WITTIG-[1,2]- AND [2,3]-REARRANGEMENT
summary: Application page for Wittig [1,2]- and [2,3]-rearrangements showing stereocontrolled alcohol and homoallylic alcohol formation in zaragozic acid A, astrophylline, sarcophytol A, and prostaglandin intermediate synthesis.
notes: Examples emphasize chirality transfer, organostannane-based Still variants, and macrocyclic allylic ether rearrangements.

title: WOHL-ZIEGLER BROMINATION
summary: Overview page for the Wohl-Ziegler bromination describing NBS-mediated radical bromination at allylic or benzylic positions, typically under thermal or photochemical initiation while minimizing direct alkene bromination.
notes: Covers the roles of NBS, radical initiators, low bromine concentration, and selectivity for allylic or benzylic C-H abstraction.

title: WOHL-ZIEGLER BROMINATION
summary: Application page for Wohl-Ziegler bromination highlighting allylic or benzylic bromide installation in the syntheses of mastigophorene C, CMP-Kdo synthetase inhibitors, tryprostatin A, and conformationally restricted tubulin inhibitors.
notes: Examples show NBS bromination of complex alkenes and methylindoles, followed by hydrolysis, Staudinger reduction, or amination.

title: WOLFF REARRANGEMENT
summary: Overview page for the Wolff rearrangement describing conversion of alpha-diazoketones into ketenes under thermal, photochemical, or transition-metal-promoted conditions, including links to Arndt-Eistert homologation and ketene trapping chemistry.
notes: Emphasizes alpha-diazoketone preparation, ketene intermediacy, ring contraction, and the influence of photolysis or metal catalysis on rearrangement efficiency.

title: WOLFF REARRANGEMENT
summary: Application page for the Wolff rearrangement showcasing diazoketone photolysis and ketene generation in the syntheses of campherenone, capnellene, oxetanocin, and salvilenone.
notes: Examples feature photochemical rearrangement of cyclic diazoketones, ring contraction, and tandem ketene capture or annulation sequences.
